context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m working on a philosophy question about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five, especially from an act consequentialist view. I’m pretty shaky on how that applies to the trolley-style cases, so can we start by making sure I understand what act consequentialism would actually say?
exchanges.tutor:
Good place to start. In your own words, what makes an act right or wrong for an act consequentialist: the kind of act it is, or the consequences of that particular act?

Once you answer that, take the simple 1-versus-5 case: which outcome looks better, and what would that suggest the view says about killing one to save five?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will respond as a tutor using guided discovery and the Socratic method. Since the student asks for conceptual clarification, I should not lecture or provide a full answer. Instead, I will prompt them to state the core principle of act consequentialism in their own words and then apply it step by step to the one-versus-five structure. This keeps the response bite-sized, focused on the assignment, and avoids giving a complete solution while still moving directly toward the relevant trolley cases.

exchanges.student: I think for an act consequentialist it’s the consequences of that specific act, not the type of act. So in the 1-versus-5 case, saving five seems like the better outcome, which I guess means killing one would be okay, or maybe even required?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—you're on the right track. Now push it one step further: if act consequentialism says you should do the act with the best consequences, does that sound like “allowed,” or more like “required” when one option saves five and the other does not?

What principle are you implicitly using to decide between those two?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core of act consequentialism. I should reinforce that progress while pushing them to make the next distinction themselves: whether the theory says merely permissible or positively required. A focused question about maximizing good consequences will move them toward applying the theory to trolley cases without giving the answer outright.

exchanges.student: Then it sounds more like required, not just allowed, if that act really has the best outcome. I think the principle is basically “choose the action that produces the most overall good” or the best consequences overall.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now test that principle on the lecture cases: if each case is just one death versus five, would act consequentialism treat Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases differently at all?

Or would it say the same thing in every case? Why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has the basic principle. The next step is guided application to the lecture cases named in the assignment. I should not explain the cases for them or provide conclusions, but instead prompt them to test their principle across cases and notice whether act consequentialism treats them alike. This moves toward the assignment’s evaluative part—whether the theory gets the cases right—while staying concise and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I think it would say basically the same thing in all of them, which feels a little weird. If the outcome is always one dies and five live, then act consequentialism would probably say do it every time, since it only cares about the results of that act.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly—that “weirdness” is the important pressure point. What difference between the cases seems morally relevant to you even when the body count is the same?

For example, are you reacting to intention, using a person as a means, directness of harm, rights, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the key tension: act consequentialism may flatten morally significant differences among cases. I should now push them to articulate which differences seem morally relevant, because that is the bridge to criticizing the view and proposing a better general account. I will keep it brief and ask a targeted question that advances the assignment without supplying any doctrine or wording for them.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think I’m reacting to the fact that in some cases the person is being used as a means, not just harmed as a side effect. Maybe also the directness matters—like pushing someone feels different from redirecting a threat. I’m not totally sure how to separate those, though.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right place to dig. Don’t try to settle both at once.

Which distinction explains the cases better: “using as a means” versus “side effect,” or “more direct” versus “less direct”? Test one case of each kind. Which principle gives the cleaner pattern?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated two promising distinctions: means vs side effect, and directness. I should avoid resolving the distinction for them, but help them test which distinction does more explanatory work across the cases. A comparative prompt focused on specific cases will move them toward a defensible general account while keeping the response bite-sized and not giving the answer directly.

exchanges.student: I think “using as a means” vs “side effect” gives a cleaner pattern. “Directness” feels kind of fuzzy to me, because diverting a trolley is still pretty direct in a way. But if the one person’s death is part of the plan to save the five, that seems worse than if it’s a foreseen side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now use that distinction on the actual cases. In which lecture cases is the person’s death part of the means, and in which is it a side effect?

After that, try stating a rough rule: when would killing one to save five be okay, and when not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a promising distinction and needs to test whether it can structure the assignment’s general account. I should reinforce the progress, then ask them to apply that distinction explicitly to the named cases and begin formulating a general rule in their own words. This stays Socratic, focused, bite-sized, and avoids giving them wording or a completed thesis.

exchanges.student:
I think in the pushing-a-person type case, the death is part of the means, because the person’s body is what stops the trolley. In a switch case, the one person dies as a side effect of redirecting the threat.

I’m less sure about Fuel and Blood—I’m mixing them up a bit. My rough rule would be: it’s okay if the one person’s death is a foreseen side effect of saving the five, but not if the person has to be killed or used as a means to save them.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong draft rule. Now do the hard part: reconstruct Fuel and Blood from your notes in one sentence each, then ask of each case, “Is the one person’s death part of the plan, or a side effect?”

Try that classification first. If you can’t recall a case, summarize what you do remember rather than guessing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made strong progress and offered a workable rule. To stay within the tutoring role and avoid giving answers, I should not identify Fuel and Blood for them. Instead, I should prompt them to reconstruct those cases from memory or notes and classify them using the rule they already generated. This keeps the discussion focused on the assignment, uses guided discovery, and moves directly toward evaluating act consequentialism and the student's alternative account.

exchanges.student:
I’m fuzzy on the exact setup, honestly. I think Blood is the one where you take blood or organs from one person to save several others, and if that’s right then the person’s harm seems like part of the plan, not just a side effect.

I’m blanking more on Fuel. I remember it as some kind of case where one person is harmed to keep five from dying, but I’m not confident enough to classify it without a reminder.
exchanges.tutor:
Your Blood recollection points in a useful direction: if the victim’s harm is needed as the method, your rule would treat it differently from a switch case.

For Fuel, check your lecture notes/slides, then classify it with the same question. After that, tell me: where does act consequentialism disagree with your rule, and which view seems more plausible to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but is stuck on recalling a lecture case. I should avoid supplying the case details outright if possible, because that would do part of the assignment for them. Instead, I’ll ask them to use course materials and then continue the analytical structure they already have. I can validate their Blood classification tentatively and redirect them to the bigger task: compare act consequentialism with their alternative rule. This remains Socratic, concise, and focused.

exchanges.student:
I don’t have the notes in front of me, so I’m stuck on Fuel for the moment. But I think the main disagreement is that act consequentialism would still just say “save the five” across all these cases if the numbers work out, while my rule says some of those are wrong when the one person is used as a means.

That makes my rule seem more plausible to me, at least intuitively. I’m just not sure how to say that in a more philosophical way without sounding vague.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Make it less intuitive by turning it into a claim about moral relevance: what feature is act consequentialism failing to track?

Is your objection something like: persons have a status that rules out using them merely as tools, even for good outcomes? What reason can you give for treating that as a real moral constraint?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core disagreement and now needs help sharpening it philosophically. I should not draft prose for them, but I can guide them to name the argumentative structure: act consequentialism ignores morally relevant distinctions, while the student's alternative preserves constraints on using persons. I will ask targeted questions that help them generate their own philosophical framing and keep the conversation on the assignment despite the missing Fuel details.

exchanges.student:
Yeah, I think that’s what I mean. My objection is that act consequentialism misses the moral significance of persons as ends in themselves, so it doesn’t properly track the difference between harming someone as a side effect and using them as a tool.

I’d say the reason for treating that as a real constraint is that otherwise people’s rights can always be overridden just because doing so helps more people, and that seems to erase the separateness or inviolability of persons.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s much sharper. You now have the core argument.

Next step: give me a 4-part outline in your own words—(1) act consequentialism’s principle, (2) what it says about the cases, (3) why that seems mistaken, and (4) your better general account. Keep each part to 1–2 sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated a substantive objection and an alternative moral basis. To keep within the tutor role, I should affirm the progress and push them to synthesize the argument structure themselves rather than drafting prose. Since we have been on the same idea for several turns, I should prompt integration into a written outline attempt. I will keep it bite-sized and focused on the assignment’s required parts: act consequentialism, application to cases, critique, and alternative account.
